title: Optimized extraction, quantification and characterization of seven environmentally relevant metallic nanoparticles in sewage sludge: Occurrence, composition and environmental risk assessment
author: Lučić, Mavro
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
Quantification of Seven Environmentally Relevant Metallic Nanoparticles in Sewage Sludge: sp-ICP-MS Dataset  
 This dataset contains single-particle inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (sp-ICP-MS) measurements of seven metallic nanoparticles extracted from sewage sludge samples collected from five wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) in Croatia. The data support the research article "Optimized extraction, quantification and characterization of seven environmentally relevant metallic nanoparticles in sewage sludge: Occurrence, composition and environmental risk assessment" published in the Journal of Hazardous Materials (2025). 
  DOI of associated publication: 10.1016/j.jhazmat.2025.139734 
  Sample Information  Wastewater Treatment Plants: 
 ZG - Zagreb WWTP (primary method development site) VG - Velika Gorica WWTP CAK - Čakovec WWTP KAR - Karlovac WWTP SIS - Sisak WWTP 
 Sampling period: 2023 Sample preparation: Air-dried and sieved through a 2-mm mesh Geographic location: Croatia 
  Methodology  Extraction Protocol Optimal extraction solution: 2.5 mM tetrasodium pyrophosphate (TSPP) 
 Sludge-to-reagent ratio: 1:100 (200 mg sludge in 20 mL solution) Shaking: 24 hours at 300 rpm on orbital shaker Sonication: 20 minutes in water bath Settling time: 2 hours 30 minutes for TSPP solutions Physical separation: Gravitational settling (no centrifugation to prevent loss of heavier particles) 
 Analytical Technique Instrument: Agilent 7900 ICP-MS (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA) 
  sp-ICP-MS parameters:  
 Dwell time: 100 µs Sample flow rate: ~0.33 mL/min (0.1 rps peristaltic pump speed) Transport efficiency: Determined daily using 53 nm Au NPs (~11%) Acquisition time: 60 seconds per sample Target particle events: 100-3800 per measurement 
 Isotopes monitored: 
 107Ag (no gas mode) 197Au (no gas mode) 140Ce (no gas mode) 63Cu (He collision mode, 4.3 mL/min) 208Pb (no gas mode) 47Ti (no gas mode) 66Zn (He collision mode, 4.3 mL/min) 
 Data processing software: spCal (open-source Python-based platform) 
 Filtering: Poisson-based (α=0.01) to differentiate particle signals from background Particle size calculation: Spherical geometry assumption with element-specific densities 
 Quality Control Particle recovery metrics (using 2.5 mM TSPP): 
 Particle number recovery: >75% for all validated nanoparticles Particle mass recovery: >84.5% for all validated nanoparticles Mean diameter recovery: 84.5-108.4% (indicates minimal transformation) 
 Validation: Quintuplicate measurements for final protocol validation 
  Data Structure  The Excel file (MNP_WWTP.xlsx) contains the following information: Columns include: 
 WWTP identifier (ZG, VG, CAK, KAR, SIS) Nanoparticle type (Ag, Au, Ce, Cu, Pb, Ti, Zn) Particle number concentration (particles/g dry sludge) Particle mass concentration (µg/g dry sludge) Particle size distribution data (mean diameter in nm) Total mass concentration (particulate + dissolved fraction) Standard deviations for replicate measurements (n=5) 
  Key findings:  
 Extracted metallic nanoparticles represented <2% of total metal content for most elements Highest extraction efficiency observed for Ti NPs (up to 10.3%) and Cu NPs (up to 4.4%) VG (Velika Gorica) samples showed highest extraction efficiency, likely due to absence of anaerobic digestion treatment 
 Physicochemical Characterization Sludge samples were characterized for: 
 Particle size distribution (laser diffraction, Beckman Coulter LS 13320) Organic matter content (loss on ignition method, 360°C for 2 hours) Total elemental composition (microwave digestion followed by ICP-MS/MS analysis) Quality control verified using certified reference materials (Soil NCS DC 773902, GBW 7410) with 90-100% recoveries 
 Complementary Analyses Electron microscopy: Scanning electron microscopy coupled with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (SEM-EDS, Verios G4 HP with Ultim Max detector) was performed on selected supernatants to characterize nanoparticle morphology and chemical composition. 
   Key SEM-EDS findings:  
 Ag NPs predominantly present as AgS with Ca and P associations Ti NPs showed simple composition indicating anthropogenic origin (cosmetics, paints) Cu NPs revealed multiple sources (industrial copper-chromium oxides in CAK) Pb NPs exhibited associations with S and Cl (forming PbS or chloropyromorphite) Zn NPs predominantly occurred as ZnS with Na, K, and Ca associations Ce-containing particles displayed rare earth element signatures (La, Ce, Pr, Nd, Sm, Gd) with thorium and phosphorus, indicative of monazite or processed REE-phosphate materials 
 Environmental Significance Enrichment Factor Analysis: Comparison of sludge concentrations to background soil levels revealed: 
 High anthropogenic enrichment: Ag (EF ~40-250), Pb (EF ~70-90 in Zagreb) Moderate anthropogenic enrichment: Zn (EF ~9.0-31.5), Cu (EF ~12.3-35.1) Slight enrichment: Ti (EF >1.5 for ZG and VG) Natural origin: Ce (EF ~1.0) 
 Environmental Risk Assessment: Preliminary risk quotient (RQ) calculations based on predicted environmental concentrations (assuming 1.6 tons sludge/hectare application rate) and predicted no-effect concentrations from literature indicated: 
 Negligible risk (RQ <0.01): Au, Ce, Ti nanoparticles Low risk (0.01 ≤ RQ <0.1): Ag, Cu, Pb, Zn nanoparticles Highly soluble species (Ag and Zn NPs) may pose greater long-term environmental risks through enhanced leaching 
 Particulate vs. dissolved fractions (ratio of particle mass to total mass): 
 Most stable: Ti (2.48), Ce (0.68) Intermediate: Pb (0.42), Au (0.34), Cu (0.23) Highly soluble: Zn (0.09), Ag (0.04) 
  Methodological Innovations  
 First-time quantification of Pb nanoparticles in solid environmental matrices using sp-ICP-MS Simultaneous multi-element screening of seven metallic nanoparticles from a single extraction Critical time-dependent dissolution discovery for Ag and CuO NPs, requiring immediate extraction (achievement of 96.5-108.2% number recovery vs. 35.2% with 24-hour incubation) Systematic comparison of extraction solutions (TSPP at 2.5-20 mM, TMAH at 5-25%, milli-Q water) across multiple particle metrics (number, mass, and size recovery) 
  Limitations and Considerations  
 Method optimized specifically for municipal sewage sludge with high organic matter content May require validation for different environmental matrices (soils, sediments, industrial sludge) ZnO nanoparticles could not be reliably quantified due to extensive dissolution Regional validation is recommended for sludge from different geographic areas with varying industrial inputs 2.5 mM TSPP represents an optimal compromise across multiple nanoparticle types, but may not be ideal for individual elements at higher TSPP concentrations 
  Contact Information   Mavro Lučić, PhD Division for Marine and Environmental Research Ruđer Bošković Institute Bijenička cesta 54, 10000 Zagreb, Croatia Email: mlucic@irb.hr  This research was supported by: 
 Slovenian Research and Innovation Agency – ARIS Program P1–0143 European Union – NextGenerationEU program (Mavro Lučić's postdoctoral fellowship at Jožef Stefan Institute) 
 Keywords metallic nanoparticles, sp-ICP-MS, sewage sludge, environmental monitoring, extraction optimization, silver nanoparticles, copper nanoparticles, lead nanoparticles, titanium nanoparticles, zinc nanoparticles, gold nanoparticles, cerium nanoparticles, wastewater treatment, TSPP extraction, Croatia, environmental risk assessment, emerging contaminants, nanomaterial quantification
